questions.question: The 'Naive' in Naive Bayes means:
options: Features are assumed conditionally independent given the class
questions.explanation: Naive Bayes assumes P(x1,x2|y) = P(x1|y) * P(x2|y). This independence assumption is often violated but the model still works well in practice.

questions.question: MultinomialNB is best for:
options: Text classification with word frequency counts
questions.explanation: MultinomialNB models word count distributions, making it ideal for document classification tasks.

questions.question: LDA serves dual purposes as:
questions.explanation: LDA finds directions that maximise class separation, both for classification and projecting to lower dimensions.

questions.question: Laplace smoothing prevents:
questions.explanation: Without smoothing, a word unseen in training gives probability 0, zeroing out the entire posterior. Laplace adds a small constant.

questions.question: GaussianNB assumes features follow:
questions.explanation: GaussianNB models continuous features as normally distributed within each class.

questions.question: LDA assumes:
options: All classes share the same covariance matrix
questions.explanation: LDA (Fisher's) assumes equal class covariance. When this is violated, QDA (Quadratic DA) is preferred.

questions.question: Naive Bayes works well with text because:
options: Word counts are large sparse vectors where the independence assumption is practically useful
questions.explanation: Despite the false independence assumption, NB handles high-dimensional sparse text features efficiently and generalises well.

questions.question: BernoulliNB is most appropriate when:
questions.explanation: BernoulliNB models binary features — suitable for bag-of-words where you only care whether a word appears, not how often.

questions.question: Compared to logistic regression, Naive Bayes is generally:
options: Faster to train but less accurate when independence assumption is violated
questions.explanation: NB is very fast and effective when features are roughly independent. LR usually outperforms NB when features are correlated.

questions.question: QDA (Quadratic DA) differs from LDA by:
options: Allowing each class to have its own covariance matrix
questions.explanation: QDA relaxes LDA's equal-covariance assumption, allowing quadratic boundaries — more flexible but needs more data.
